title: Monetary and Fiscal Policy in HANK
overview: This intensive course introduces graduate students, academics, and practitioners to the analysis of monetary and fiscal policy in heterogeneous-agent New Keynesian (HANK) models. The course explores the empirical and theoretical motivations for moving beyond representative-agent frameworks in macroeconomic policy analysis and focuses on the core policy mechanisms, the interpretation of model results, and the comparison between quantitative HANK models and tractable alternatives such as TANK (two-agents NK model) and THANK (tractable HANK model). The course combines intuition, analytical structure, quantitative analysis, and policy applications. Participants will study how heterogeneity in marginal propensities to consume, balance-sheet positions, and income risk changes the transmission of interest-rate policy, transfers, taxation, and government spending. The course also highlights when tractable models can deliver useful policy insights and when a full quantitative HANK framework is needed.
learningOutcomes:
By the end of the course, participants will be able to:
  • Explain why household heterogeneity matters for the transmission of monetary and fiscal policy.
  • Identify and interpret the main HANK transmission channels, including redistribution, cash-flow, and earnings-heterogeneity mechanisms.
  • Assess how transfers, taxes, government spending, and interest-rate changes affect aggregate demand in the presence of heterogeneous households.
  • Understand the structure of a quantitative HANK model and the logic of sequence-space Jacobian methods used to solve and analyze it.
  • Solve a canonical one-asset HANK model in Python and interpret its quantitative policy implications.
  • Compare the insights from HANK, TANK, and THANK models and evaluate when tractable approaches are sufficient for policy analysis.
  • Connect model-based insights to current research and real-world policy questions faced by central banks, finance ministries, and other institutions.
topics: Empirical motivation for HANK: heterogeneity in income, assets, debt positions, and marginal propensities to consume.
Monetary policy transmission with heterogeneous agents: redistribution, intertemporal substitution, cash-flow effects, and balance-sheet channels.
Fiscal policy in HANK: transfers, taxes, government spending, automatic stabilizers, and fiscal multipliers.
Sufficient-statistics and sequence-space perspectives on policy transmission.
The anatomy of a quantitative HANK model: household block, incomplete markets, nominal rigidities, and equilibrium.
Reading and interpreting impulse responses and policy counterfactuals in quantitative HANK applications.
Tractable heterogeneity: TANK and THANK models as analytical benchmarks.
When tractable models are enough, and when a richer quantitative HANK framework is required.
references: Auclert, A. (2019). Monetary Policy and the Redistribution Channel. American Economic Review, 109(6), 2333-2367.
Auclert, A. (2025). HANK: A New Core of Usable Macroeconomics. AEA Papers and Proceedings, 115, 153-157.

title: Causal Inference for Macroeconomic Shocks
overview:
How do we credibly measure the effects of monetary policy, fiscal shocks, energy shocks, or uncertainty shocks when the shocks themselves are not directly observed and macroeconomic variables are jointly determined over time?

This intensive course introduces graduate students and practitioners to modern methods for identifying and estimating the dynamic causal effects of macroeconomic shocks. The course focuses on two core empirical workhorses of contemporary macroeconomics—structural vector autoregressions (SVARs) and local projections (LPs)—and on the identification strategies that make causal interpretation possible, including zero restrictions, sign restrictions, external instruments, and narrative information.

The course begins with the foundations of reduced-form VARs and the classical identification problem, then progressively introduces the main strategies that the literature has developed to move from correlation to causation. A key theme throughout is that different identification strategies encode different types of prior economic knowledge, and that the choice of method should be guided by the nature of the available information about the shock of interest.

The final sessions extend the analysis to LP–IV and to high-dimensional settings with richer information sets. Practical implementation in Python is integrated throughout. A recurring theme is how LP and SVAR estimators relate, when they coincide, and how to choose between them in practice.
learningOutcomes:
By the end of the course, participants will be able to:
  • Formulate the identification problem for structural VARs and explain why reduced-form evidence is insufficient for causal inference;
  • Implement and critically evaluate zero restrictions (short-run and long-run), understanding the economic assumptions they encode;
  • Apply sign restrictions to set-identify structural shocks and interpret the resulting sets of impulse responses;
  • Use external instruments (proxy variables) to identify structural shocks via SVAR-IV methods, and assess their relevance and credibility;
  • Incorporate narrative information as restrictions on structural shocks and historical decompositions to sharpen identification;
  • Estimate impulse response functions via local projections and LP–IV, and compare them with SVAR-based estimates;
  • Choose between SVAR- and LP-based estimators in light of the identification strategy, finite-sample bias–variance trade-off, and inferential goals. Assess and communicate uncertainty using appropriate inference methods, including HAC inference, joint confidence bands, and weak-IV-robust reasoning where relevant; and
  • Discuss the challenges of extending SVAR and LP methods to high-dimensional settings, including factor-based, penalized, and shrinkage approaches.
topics: Reduced-form VARs, the structural identification problem, and point identification through zero restrictions
Set identification through sign restrictions and mixed point/set identification strategies
Proxy variables and external instruments in SVAR-IV and LP–IV settings
Narrative restrictions and the combination of multiple identification strategies
Local projections, LP–IV, LP–VAR equivalence, and current best practice for inference and estimator choice
Extensions to high-dimensional settings: factor-augmented methods, penalized estimation, and recent inference tools for dynamic causal analysis
references: Antolín-Díaz, J., & Rubio-Ramírez, J. F. (2018). Narrative sign restrictions for SVARs. American Economic Review, 108(10), 2802–2829. https://doi.org/10.1257/aer.20161852

title: Balance Sheet Policies in DGE Models
overview: This course examines modern monetary economics with a focus on the interaction between fiscal policy, central bank balance sheets, and macroeconomic dynamics in Dynamic General Equilibrium environments. It begins with the theoretical foundations of dynamic macroeconomic models, including the inter-temporal budget constraint and the transversality condition, which underpin government solvency and asset pricing. The course then explores the Fiscal Theory of the Price Level and Wallace neutrality to understand how the composition and valuation of government liabilities can influence price determination and the effectiveness of monetary policy. Building on these foundations, the course analyzes the role of unconventional monetary policy tools—such as large-scale asset purchases and helicopter money—and examines the conditions under which such policies may break neutrality results. Finally, the course studies monetary transmission in heterogeneous-agent frameworks, particularly Tractable HANK models, and investigates how balance-sheet adjustments and deleveraging during financial crises affect aggregate demand, financial stability, and policy effectiveness.
learningOutcomes:
By the end of the course, students should be able to:
  • Derive and interpret the inter-temporal budget constraint and the transversality condition in dynamic macroeconomic models.
  • Analyze the implications of the transversality condition for price level determination within the Fiscal Theory of the Price Level, and understand the critique to the FTPL.
  • Explain the Wallace irrelevance proposition and identify the assumptions under which open market operations are irrelevant.
  • Evaluate the mechanisms through which unconventional open market operations and central bank balance-sheet policies can affect the macroeconomy.
  • Distinguish helicopter money from other forms of monetary and fiscal policy and assess its theoretical and policy implications.
  • Understand the structure and policy transmission mechanisms of heterogeneous-agent New Keynesian models, particularly T-HANK frameworks.
  • Analyze how financial frictions, leverage, and balance-sheet adjustments shape macroeconomic outcomes during deleveraging crises.
  • Critically assess the interaction between monetary policy, fiscal policy, and financial stability in modern macroeconomic environments.
topics: Transversality condition and the inter-temporal budget constraint
Fiscal Theory of the Price Level
Wallace Irrelevance
(Non-) Neutrality of Unconventional Open Market Operations
Helicopter Money
Unconventional Monetary Policy in a T-HANK environment
Balance-sheet response to deleveraging crises
references: Benigno, P. (2025). Monetary Economics and Policy: A Foundation for Modern Currency Systems. Princeton University Press.
Benigno, P. and S. Nisticò, (2020). "Non-Neutrality of Open Market Operations," American Economic Journal: Macroeconomics, 12(3): 175-226.

title: Machine Learning for Economics
overview:
This intensive course introduces graduate students and practitioners to modern machine learning methods, with a focus on applications in economics and finance. Given the breadth of topics covered, the course is necessarily selective and emphasizes intuition and practical understanding over technical details. The goal is to help participants develop a map of the field and sufficient working knowledge to use current tools and applications.

The course combines foundational topics in supervised learning with an introduction to more advanced methods, including neural networks, natural language processing (NLP), and large language models (LLMs). It also adopts a hands-on perspective, using Python (PyTorch) to illustrate (i) how machine learning models can be built and applied in practice, and (ii) how LLMs can be used effectively as coding assistants (claude-code) to accelerate workflow and experimentation.
learningOutcomes:
By the end of the course, participants will be able to:
  • Explain the main ideas behind supervised learning, including prediction, overfitting, regularization, and model evaluation.
  • Compare classical machine learning methods, including linear models, regularization methods, and tree-based approaches, in applied prediction settings.
  • Describe the basic logic of neural networks, including gradient descent, backpropagation, and automatic differentiation.
  • Discuss how modern methods for text analysis evolved from word embeddings to transformers and large language models.
  • Implement and interpret simple machine learning models in Python for applications in economics and finance.
topics: Supervised learning and model evaluation
Regularization, trees, and ensemble methods
Optimization and neural networks
Deep learning in practice with Python and PyTorch
Natural language processing, transformers, and large language models
references: James, G., Witten, D., Hastie, T., & Tibshirani, R. (2021). An Introduction to Statistical Learning with Applications in Python (2023). Springer. https://hastie.su.domains/ISLP/ISLP_website.pdf.download.html
